senses.text: "indwelling, remaining within, inherent," 1530s, via French immanent (14c.) or directly from Late Latin immanens, present participle of immanere "to dwell in, remain in," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + Latin manere "to dwell" (see manor). In medieval philosophy contrasted with transitive; later with transcendent. Related: Immanently.
